Act as a research-level mathematical proof author iterating on a
written deliverable in an Author/Critic loop. You have produced an
initial version of three canonical files; a Critic has reviewed them.
Your job each round is to refine the files in response to the Critic's
findings.

You have two tools:
  - **code_interpreter**: a Python sandbox with TeX Live + standard
    scientific libs. Run ``pdflatex`` to verify compile, and use
    sympy or numpy for computational exploration, verification and
    sanity checks. The sandbox has NO outbound network — use
    web_search_preview for any external URL or paper.
  - **web_search_preview**: free-form web search.

Your workspace lives at ``/mnt/data/`` and contains three canonical
files. The user message lists, for each, a read-only input path
(holding the previous round's contents) and a canonical write path
(``/mnt/data/<name>``) where you must write any updates.

How to update a file:
  1. Read its current contents from the listed read-only path.
  2. Build the new contents.
  3. Write to the canonical write path with
     ``Path("/mnt/data/<name>").write_text(...)``. Do not write to
     ``.new``/``.bak``/sibling paths — only canonical write paths are
     picked up.
  4. Compile-check answer.tex with ``pdflatex`` before claiming
     readiness.

A file you do not write at the canonical write path is treated as
unchanged this round.

**``research_notes.tex`` is your persistent scratchpad across rounds.**
Accumulate definitions, intermediate lemmas, computational sanity-checks,
alternative-approach sketches, dead ends you ruled out, literature notes
— anything you'd want to refer back to in a later round. **Do not turn
it into a changelog or a reply to the Critic** — that is what your
free-text thinking summary is for. A future Author turn should be able
to read research_notes.tex and pick up where it left off.

You may also emit three control blocks in your reply text:

  - **<council>...question...</council>** — invoke the Advisory
    Council on a specific sub-question. At most one per round. Use
    the Council when you are stuck or want a different angle: point
    it at specific side-problems or issues uncovered by the Critic,
    ask it to suggest a portfolio of potential approaches, or get a
    second opinion on a strategic choice. The primary purpose is
    *not* additional verification — that is handled in the parallel
    Critic call. Council members are independent strong models
    (Pro, Opus, Gemini); their replies arrive at the start of your
    next turn.

  - **<compute_agent>...instructions...</compute_agent>** —
    commission an out-of-band codex CLI worker for one focused task
    per round. At most one per round. Soft timeout 60 min, full
    network access, persistent workspace where ``code/``, ``data/``,
    ``papers/``, ``notes/`` survive across rounds, and — depending
    on the sandbox image — possibly a richer CAS toolchain (e.g.
    SageMath, GAP, Singular, PARI/GP). The worker can probe what is actually
    installed, so you don't need to know in advance; ask it to
    verify availability before committing to a particular tool.
    Best use is heavy computation or multi-round code development
    that would be too slow or too large for your own code_interpreter
    (which is ephemeral, has no network, and times out per cell),
    symbolic-algebra work that benefits from a real CAS rather than
    ``sympy``, or deeper literature retrieval (downloading actual
    PDFs / TeX sources from arXiv etc.). Each invocation the worker
    re-sees a fresh read-only snapshot of your three canonical
    files; it writes findings to
    ``responses/response_round_N.md`` and the whole workspace is
    zipped and attached to your *next* turn as a read-only file you
    can ``unzip`` via code_interpreter to inspect logs, data, and
    downloaded papers. The reply arrives at the start of your next
    turn, alongside any council replies. Give it specific, ordered,
    executable instructions — it does not see prior loop history
    beyond what you tell it and what is in its own persistent
    workspace.

  - **<ready>true</ready>** — signal that you believe the answer is
    ready for submission. See the readiness rules below.

Anything outside those control blocks is treated as a brief free-text
"thinking summary" for the human reviewer and the Critic. Do NOT
paste file contents in your reply.

---

Research ambition and problem interpretation. Your goal is to resolve
the given question to the fullest extent possible, with the ideal
outcome being a complete and rigorous solution. The solution may
require novel, creative, and non-trivial elements. If a non-trivial
lemma, computation, or reduction appears necessary, attempt to prove
the lemma, run the computation, or carry out the reduction in this turn
before listing it as a gap. Do not stop at "this remains to be proved"
while you still have a plausible route to attack it.

If the problem statement is ambiguous, inconsistent, missing
definitions, or likely contains a typo, begin answer.tex with a short
section titled "Problem statement and interpretation". State the
cleanest faithful correction or restatement, list any assumptions, and
do not silently solve a different problem. Record the same
interpretation in research_notes.tex so later rounds preserve it. If
the problem is already well-defined, no extra interpretation section is
needed.

When open gaps remain in your treatment, end answer.tex with a section
titled "Remaining open issues" (or equivalent). For each gap, state:
(a) what it is mathematically; (b) where in the document it appears;
(c) what was tried this round or earlier; (d) what would be needed to
close it (a specific computation, an alternative approach, a missing
reference, etc.). If rounds remain and any essential mathematical gap
is still open, keep working by focusing on closing that gap or
exploring an alternative approach to the problem.

Readiness rule. Declare ``<ready>true</ready>`` only when you
genuinely believe the answer is a complete rigorous solution to the
stated problem, with no remaining open gaps, no unproved essential
lemmas, and no missing assumptions. Do not declare
``<ready>true</ready>`` merely because the last round has been reached
or because a "Remaining open issues" section honestly lists what is
left; partial final outputs may list open issues, but they are not
ready. Even then the run terminates only if the Critic also concurs.

### Problem ###
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

This run is resumed from an earlier workflow that terminated at round 15. The new upper round bound is round 20; continue the existing draft and round history instead of restarting the solution.

Additional instructions for this continuation:
The previous pass did not reach Author/Critic agreement. Continue the existing run up to round 20; focus on resolving the remaining Critic objections instead of restarting.

### Latest Critic review ###
## Stateful reviewer's report

## Referee report on revised `answer.tex`

### LaTeX / contract check

I reconstructed the submitted `answer.tex` and ran `pdflatex` twice. It compiles successfully to a 6-page PDF. The document uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses only the permitted `fullpage` package for layout, and I found no prohibited margin, line-spacing, or in-document font-size changes. The First Proof LaTeX contract is satisfied.

### Overall verdict

The revision substantially improves the previous draft. In particular, the previously problematic normal-support identification, direct-sum support argument, fusion-label induction, compact-corner hypertrace step, and Bass–Serre reduced-word argument have all been expanded enough to remove the prior fatal gaps. I find no remaining mathematical issue that affects validity.

## Section-by-section audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The proof explicitly records the adopted interpretation of irreducibility as “not a non-trivial join,” equivalently connected complement graph. This is the standard graph-product interpretation, and it matches the use of CDHJKN’s join-irreducible results later in the proof. The problem statement ambiguity is therefore handled.

### Normal support bookkeeping

The new Lemma `normal support bookkeeping` addresses the previous main objection. It now separates the ordinary open support of \(X\) from that of \(K_X(N)\), proves that their compressed normal supports agree, and formulates the annihilator characterization explicitly.

This is supported by DKEP’s normal-bidual construction: DKEP describe \((A^\sharp)^*\) as the normal-support corner \(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), and for a subalgebra \(B\subset A\) whose multiplier action is faithful they identify \((B^\sharp)^*\) with the corresponding compressed support corner. The simultaneous \(N,JNJ\)-normal version is exactly the role of Proposition 2.10. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The equality of the supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) is now justified via DKEP Theorem 5.6 and Proposition 3.6: DKEP identify \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) as the relevant \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure and characterize it by annihilation against \(\sharp_J\)-normal functionals vanishing on \(X B(L^2N) X\); since \(X\) is hereditary, vanishing on \(X\) gives vanishing on that product. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

This resolves the previous support-calculus objection.

### Subalgebra coefficients

The proof that the coefficient boundary of
\[
{}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N
\]
is \(X_B\) is now acceptable. It uses DKEP Example 5.11, which defines coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences and identifies the subalgebra boundary in the case of \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The argument that \(Z\subset U\Rightarrow X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\) via \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\) is correct.

### Direct sums and finite fusions

The direct-sum lemma now contains the missing finite-subset net argument, boundedness control for coordinate truncations, and the correct appeal to DKEP Theorem 5.10 and Proposition 5.9. DKEP Theorem 5.10 gives the equivalence between mixing of correspondences and relative compactness of all bounded-vector coefficient operators; Proposition 5.9 converts rectangular relative compactness into \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\in K_X(N)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The finite-fusion algebra argument is also now adequate. The coefficient product formula
\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
=
T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}
\]
is consistent with DKEP’s Connes-fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The construction of a contractive approximate unit from functions \(g_\varepsilon(s)=s(s+\varepsilon)^{-1}\), and the polynomial approximation argument, are standard and sufficient.

### Intersection of boundary supports

This was the central gap in the prior draft. The revised proof now gives a sufficiently explicit argument.

The proof uses CDHJKN’s identification of \(H_R(V,V)\) with
\[
L^2M\otimes_{M_R}L^2M
\]
and their fusion rule
\[
H_{U_1}(V_1,V_2)\otimes_{M_{V_2}}H_{U_2}(V_2,V_3)
\cong
\bigoplus_{W\subset U_1\cap U_2}H_W(V_1,V_3)^{\oplus k}.
\]
This directly supports the induction that a finite fusion with labels \(A_1,\dots,A_n\) decomposes over subsets of \(\cap_iA_i\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

The passage from coefficient products to support projections is also now justified: positive finite-fusion products land in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\), norm limits land in \(K_{X_{M_I}}^{\infty,1}(M)\), and the normal-support lemma then converts annihilation against \(A_M^{\sharp_J}\)-functionals into membership in the normal-bidual corner \(q_I\mathcal A_Mq_I\). This resolves the earlier nonmultiplicativity concern.

The induction for palindromic products is correct.

### Finite-boundary criterion

The finite-boundary criterion is now sound. The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) gives a central state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\) if \(\omega(q_i^\perp)>0\), and the factor assumption correctly forces its normal restriction to \(N\) to be the trace.

The previous issue with the formal expression \(q_0Tq_0\) has been fixed. DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 explicitly uses natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal compact corner, and the revised proof invokes that map instead of pretending that the concrete inclusion is multiplicative. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The final Connes-hypertrace contradiction is standard and valid.

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The revised proof gives enough AFP normal-form detail. The projections \(p,q\) onto first-letter \(P\)- and \(Q\)-subspaces are shown to lie in \(S_{X_B}(N)\) using commutator computations and DKEP Lemma 6.1. DKEP’s definition of \(S_X(M)\), Lemma 6.1, and Theorem 6.2 justify this route to relative proper proximality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
are correctly justified by reduced-word orthogonality. The \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) is also correct: \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for all \(n\ne0\), so the projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and centrality forces their common state value to be zero.

### Graph-product application

The graph-product AFP decomposition over \(M_{\Lk(v)}\) is standard and is used correctly. The construction of the unitaries \(w_1,w_2\) from connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) is correct in both cases \(C(v)\) has at least two vertices and \(C(v)=\{r\}\). The expectation computations follow from graph-product reduced-word calculus.

The final use of CDHJKN is now correctly stated. CDHJKN Theorem E gives the factoriality criterion, and their Theorem 2.4 gives the join-irreducible \(|V|\ge3\) factor case directly. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150)) CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability criterion, and connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices excludes the exceptional isolated nonedge condition. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.08150))

Thus \(M\) is a separable non-amenable finite factor, and the finite-boundary criterion applies.

## Final assessment

All previously fatal objections have been addressed:

- normal-support calculus: addressed;
- equality of supports for \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\): addressed;
- direct-sum coefficient control: addressed;
- CDHJKN fusion-label induction: addressed;
- nonmultiplicative normal-bidual compression issue: addressed;
- hypertrace step: addressed;
- Bass–Serre reduced-word details: addressed;
- final factoriality/nonamenability citations: addressed.

I find no remaining open gap or missing assumption affecting the proof.

---

## Independent fresh reviewer's report

## Referee report on `answer.tex`

### Overall verdict

`answer.tex` **compiles and satisfies the formal LaTeX contract**, but I do **not** regard it as a complete rigorous proof. The main theorem may well be true, and several cited external ingredients are directionally appropriate, but the submitted proof contains essential unproved support-calculus steps, a new relative Bass–Serre proposition proved only sketchily, and several places where DKEP results are invoked in stronger forms than the cited statements visibly provide.

### LaTeX contract check

I compiled `answer.tex` with `pdflatex` twice. It compiles successfully, uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses only the permitted `fullpage` layout package, contains no visible forbidden font-size or line-spacing changes, and produces a **6-page** PDF. Thus there is **no LaTeX-contract failure**.

### Literature cross-check

The broad external references are real and relevant. DKEP’s paper defines the normal-bidual framework and Lemma 8.5 indeed characterizes proper proximality relative to a boundary piece by absence of central states on a normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Theorem 5.6 gives an annihilator/mixing characterization, but in the form involving functionals vanishing on \(X B(L^2N,L^2M)Y\), with an additional special condition for \(T\in M(X)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP also records the fusion coefficient identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), and Example 5.11 identifies coefficient boundary pieces for correspondences. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) CDHJKN’s Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 do provide the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used in spirit. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 gives factoriality/fullness for join-irreducible graph products with at least three vertices under the state-zero-unitary hypothesis, and Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability criterion cited near the end. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

The problem is not that the cited papers are irrelevant; the problem is that `answer.tex` uses several consequences that are not stated/proved with sufficient precision.

## Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

### Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation “irreducible = not a non-trivial join = complement connected” is reasonable and should be acceptable. The answer correctly records this interpretation. Minor issue: the original problem’s wording using subgraphs is slightly imprecise about nonempty/proper/disjoint join factors, but the adopted interpretation is standard.

### Normal-bidual setup

The definition of \(\mathcal A_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*\), \(q_X\), and \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) follows DKEP’s framework. However, the answer immediately uses a very strong support-calculus model in \(A_N^{**}\), and later arguments depend critically on this model. This must be proved carefully, not just asserted.

### Lemma “normal support bookkeeping”

This is one of the main weak points.

1. The proof says DKEP Proposition 2.10 “identifies” \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) with  
   \[
   p_N\bar q_Y A_N^{**}\bar q_Yp_N.
   \]
   DKEP Proposition 2.10 visibly states an inclusion/equality of simultaneous normal biduals inside \(M(A)^{**}\), and an inclusion for subalgebras \(B\subset A\); it does not, as quoted, explicitly give the ordinary open-support formula in the form used here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) This support-corner identification may be true, but `answer.tex` does not prove it.

2. The proof claims that DKEP Theorem 5.6 implies that the annihilators of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) inside \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\) coincide. Theorem 5.6’s annihilator condition is stated for functionals vanishing on \(X B(L^2N,L^2M)Y\), and the simplified “vanishing on \(X\)” form appears only under additional hypotheses. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The answer’s argument “DKEP use this in Lemma 8.5” is not a proof of equality of normal supports.

3. The final assertion about \(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) being killed by every \(\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\) with \(\phi|_X=0\) is essential later, but it is not rigorously derived. The answer needs to show precisely why Theorem 5.6 applies to such \(T\), and why vanishing on \(X\) is enough.

This lemma is foundational for the boundary-intersection proof. As written, it is not rigorous enough.

### Lemma “subalgebra coefficients”

The conclusion is plausible, but the proof is incomplete.

DKEP Example 5.11 identifies the boundary piece generated by coefficients of a correspondence, and notes that for \(L^2M\) as an \(M\)-\(B\) correspondence one gets the subalgebra boundary piece. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` then passes to the fused correspondence
\[
{}_N L^2N_B\otimes_B {}_B L^2N_N
\]
and asserts that its coefficients lie in the hereditary algebra generated by the \(e_B\)-coefficients. The displayed identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) alone gives products with a middle coefficient \(T_\eta^*T_{\eta'}\) on \(L^2B\); it does not by itself prove membership in the hereditary algebra generated by \(e_B\). A linking-algebra or hereditary-corner argument is needed.

The conjugate identification \(\overline{\xi\otimes\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes J\xi\) is also stated without checking well-definedness on the Connes fusion quotient.

### Lemma “direct sums and finite fusions”

This lemma contains several nontrivial assertions that are not fully justified.

- The direct-sum argument applies DKEP Theorem 5.10. That theorem gives equivalences between mixing of a correspondence and membership of all bounded-vector operators in relative compact classes. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But the proof needs to check carefully which side is the \(X\)-boundary and which side is the full boundary \(A_N\). The notation in `answer.tex` is too compressed to verify this unambiguously.

- The infinite direct-sum passage uses coordinate projections and says the closedness argument in DKEP Theorem 5.10 applies. DKEP’s proof uses a specific approximation/cutdown argument for bounded vectors. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The answer does not prove that the direct-sum bounded vectors satisfy the required uniform boundedness and closure hypotheses in the exact topology used by DKEP.

- The approximate-unit statement for \(X_{M_U}\) depends on the previous “subalgebra coefficients” lemma. Since that lemma is incomplete, this one inherits the gap.

### Lemma “intersection of boundary supports”

The CDHJKN fusion input is correctly aimed: their Theorem 5.4 decomposes graph-product bimodules, and Proposition 5.5 gives fusions over intersections. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) The algebraic identity
\[
hkh=T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}^*
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}
\]
is also consistent with DKEP’s coefficient identity.

However, the proof still depends on unresolved support and annihilator assertions:

1. The step from coefficients of direct sums of \(H_C\)’s to \(hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\) depends on the direct-sum lemma, which is not fully proved.

2. The passage
   \[
   \phi(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha)=0
   \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
   \phi(\bar q_W\bar q_U\bar q_W)=0
   \]
   uses strong-* convergence in \(A_M^{**}\) and normality of \(\phi\)’s extension. This can be made valid, but the proof does not spell out the required normal-extension and iterated-limit argument.

3. The compression by \(p_M\) and the conclusion that all normal functionals annihilating \(X_{M_I}\) vanish on \(q_Wq_Uq_W\) relies exactly on Lemma 1’s support identification, which is not established rigorously.

Thus the boundary-support intersection lemma remains an essential unproved component.

### Lemma “from finitely many relative boundaries to compact boundary”

This lemma is conceptually plausible, but again has gaps.

- The compression
  \[
  y\mapsto \omega(q^\perp yq^\perp)
  \]
  is asserted to define a state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\) after observing \(q^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}q^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)\). This inclusion should be proved explicitly at the operator-system level.

- The hypertrace step invokes a u.c.p. map
  \[
  \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0
  \]
  with \(\Theta_0(1)=q_0\) and \(\Theta_0(x)=xq_0\). DKEP Lemma 8.5 does mention natural u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner, but `answer.tex` does not construct this map or justify the exact formulas used. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) Since this is the step that converts \(\omega(q_0)=1\) into a Connes hypertrace, it is essential.

### Proposition “relative Bass–Serre paradox”

This is a new substantial proposition, not directly quoted from the literature. It needs a complete proof, but the current proof is closer to a sketch.

- The AFP first-letter projection formulas are plausible, but they are not proved in detail. In amalgamated free products, cancellations into \(B\) must be handled carefully.

- The assertion that the commutators lie in \(X_B\) because they are in the \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule generated by \(e_B\) depends on the precise DKEP definition of the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\), and this is not expanded.

- The application of DKEP Lemma 6.1 is compressed. DKEP Lemma 6.1 is a closure criterion for the set of algebra elements whose commutators with a vector lie in the mixing part. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The answer should explicitly connect the checked commutators with the hypotheses of that lemma.

- The orthogonality of \(h^n e_Bh^{-n}\) is asserted from \(E_B(h^n)=0\). This is standard, but the actual calculation
  \[
  (h^m e_Bh^{-m})(h^n e_Bh^{-n})=0\quad(m\ne n)
  \]
  should be included.

Because this proposition is the mechanism proving relative proper proximality for each link, its proof must be fully rigorous. As written, it is not.

### Application to graph products

The graph-product part is mostly plausible.

- The AFP decomposition
  \[
  M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
  \]
  is standard, but no citation or proof is supplied.

- The choices of \(w_1,w_2\) are combinatorially reasonable. The vanishing expectations are dismissed as “graph-product reduced words”; this should be expanded, but it is probably fixable.

- The final use of CDHJKN factoriality and nonamenability is correct in spirit. The cited theorem is better referred to as CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 or Main Theorem 0.5, not “Theorem E.” CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 indeed gives a diffuse full factor for join-irreducible graphs with \(|V|\ge3\) and state-zero unitaries. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 indeed implies nonamenability under the connected-complement hypothesis with at least three vertices. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

## Final assessment

The submitted proof has a coherent strategy, and several major ingredients are plausible. But it is **not yet a complete rigorous solution**. The central support-calculus lemma, direct-sum coefficient lemma, boundary-intersection lemma, and relative Bass–Serre proposition all contain essential gaps or rely on stronger unstated consequences of DKEP than the text proves. These are not merely cosmetic omissions; they are load-bearing steps in the proof.

No LaTeX compile or formatting issues detected.

from pathlib import Path
answer = r"""\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\bbarotimes}{\mathbin{\overline{\otimes}}}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cF}{\mathcal F}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb C}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Irreducible is interpreted in the standard graph-product sense: \(\Gamma\) is
not a non-trivial join, equivalently the complement graph \(\Gamma^c\) is
connected.  All traces are faithful normal traces.  For \(U\subset V\),
\(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
\(E_U:M\to M_U\) its canonical expectation.

We use Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson's normal-bidual notation
\cite{DKEP}.  If \(N\) is finite, set \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^* ,
\]
the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal bidual.  If \(X\) is an
\(N\)-boundary piece, \(K_X(N)\) denotes DKEP's compact algebra associated
with \(X\), and \(q_X\in\cA_N\) is the support projection of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For the compact boundary
\(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\).  Put
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=\{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for every }
 a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For a separable finite factor, DKEP Lemma 8.5 says that proper proximality
relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the non-existence of an \(N\)-central state
on \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is the trace.

\section*{Normal supports and coefficients}

We first isolate the support bookkeeping.  If \(Y\subset A_N\) is hereditary
and \(N,JNJ\subset M(Y)\) faithfully, DKEP Proposition 2.10 embeds
\((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) as a non-unital von Neumann subalgebra of \(\cA_N\).
Let \(s(Y)\) denote its unit.

\begin{lemma}[normal support bookkeeping]\label{lem:support}
With \(Y\) as above,
\[
       (Y^{\sharp_J})^*=s(Y)\cA_Ns(Y),
\]
the canonical image of \(Y\) is weak* dense in this corner, and every
contractive approximate unit of \(Y\) converges strongly* to \(s(Y)\) in
\(\cA_N\).  Moreover, for \(\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\), the normal extension
\(\widetilde\phi\in(\cA_N)_*\) vanishes on \(s(Y)\cA_Ns(Y)\) if and only if
\(\phi|_Y=0\).

If \(X\) is a boundary piece, then \(s(X)=s(K_X(N))\).  Thus \(q_X=s(X)\), and
whenever \(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\),
\[
       \phi|_X=0,\ \phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
       \widetilde\phi(T)=0                                             \tag{2.1}
\]
in the normal-bidual sense.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For a hereditary C\(^*\)-subalgebra, the weak closure in any representation is
the corner cut out by the strong limit of any contractive approximate unit.
Applying this to the universal simultaneous normal representation used in
DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives the first assertions and the annihilator
description.

It remains to compare \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\).  If \(\phi|_X=0\), then
\(\phi\) vanishes on \(XA_NX\), since \(X\) is hereditary.  DKEP
Theorem 5.6(4), with \(M=N\) and \(X=Y\), annihilates every element of
\(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) by such a \(\phi\).  DKEP Proposition 3.6 gives
\(K_X(N)\subset K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), so \(\phi\) vanishes on \(K_X(N)\).
The converse is trivial because \(X\subset K_X(N)\).  The two hereditary
algebras therefore have the same annihilator in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\), hence the
same support in \(\cA_N\), and (2.1) is precisely the same use of Theorem 5.6.
\end{proof}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), let
\[
        \cH_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]

\begin{lemma}[subalgebra coefficients]\label{lem:coeff}
The DKEP coefficient boundary of \(\cH_B\) is exactly the subalgebra boundary
\(X_B\) of Example 3.4 in \cite{DKEP}.  In the tracial case
\(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\) as an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H={}_NL^2N_B\).  DKEP Example 5.11 identifies the left coefficient
boundary of \(H\) with \(X_B\).  For elementary bounded vectors in
\(H\otimes_B\overline H\),
\[
   T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^{\,*}T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
        =T_\xi^{\,*}\,\theta_{\alpha,\beta}\,T_\eta,                 \tag{2.2}
\]
where \(\theta_{\alpha,\beta}\in \mathbb B(L^2B)\) is the rank-one coefficient
coming from the right \(B\)-module \(H\).  Formula (2.2) is the usual
linking-algebra form of the Connes-fusion identity
\(T_{\xi\otimes\zeta}=T_\zeta T_\xi\).  DKEP Example 3.4 realizes \(X_B\) as
the hereditary algebra generated by these \(T_\xi^*\,\mathbb B(L^2B)\,T_\eta\);
hence all coefficients of \(\cH_B\) lie in \(X_B\).  Conversely, taking
\(\alpha=\beta=\widehat1\), for which \(\theta_{\alpha,\alpha}=1_{L^2B}\),
recovers the generators \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) of \(X_B\).  The conjugation
\(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes_BJ\xi\) respects the
balanced relation and is a bimodular unitary, using traciality.
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\); hence \(X_{M_Z}\subset
X_{M_U}\).

\begin{lemma}[direct sums and finite fusions]\label{lem:dsupport}
Let \(X\subset A_N\) be a boundary piece.  If
\(\cK=\bigoplus_{j\in J}\cK_j\) is a direct sum of \(N\)-\(N\) correspondences
and the coefficient boundary \(X_{\cK_j}\) is contained in \(X\) for every
\(j\), then every bounded coefficient \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) of \(\cK\) belongs to
\(K_X(N)\).

For \(U\subset V\), let \(\cF_U\) be the class of non-empty finite Connes
fusions of copies of
\[
        \cH_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M
\]
and of its conjugate.  The linear span \(\mathcal C_U\) of coefficients of
finite direct sums of members of \(\cF_U\) is a self-adjoint algebra, and
\(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive approximate unit in the norm-closure of positive
coefficients \(T_\zeta^*T_\zeta\) from such finite direct sums.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
DKEP Theorem 5.10 is applied with the full boundary on the left and \(X\) on
the right.  For each \(\cK_j\), the hypothesis \(X_{\cK_j}\subset X\) is
condition (1) of that theorem and gives \(T_\xi\in K_{A_N,X}(N,N)\) for every
bounded \(\xi\in\cK_j\).  Finite direct sums have the same property.  If
\(P_F\) is the projection onto a finite sub-sum and \(\xi\) is bounded, then
\(P_F\xi\) is bounded with the same left and right bounds and
\(P_F\xi\to\xi\) in Hilbert norm.  Hence \(T_{P_F\xi}\to T_\xi\) in DKEP's
strong module topology, uniformly on bounded sets.  The strong closedness of
the mixing operators (DKEP Proposition 5.1, used in the proof of Theorem 5.10)
gives \(T_\xi\in K_{A_N,X}(N,N)\).  Therefore
\(T_\xi^*T_\xi\in K_X(N)\) by DKEP Proposition 5.9, and polarization gives the
general coefficient.

For coefficients \(a=T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2}\) and
\(b=T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}\), the identity
\(T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha\) gives
\[
        ab=T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2},
\]
and adjoints are immediate.  Thus \(\mathcal C_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra.  By
Lemma \ref{lem:coeff}, \(X_{M_U}\) is the hereditary algebra generated by the
positive coefficients of \(\cH_U\).  If
\(s=\sum_iT_{\xi_i}^*T_{\xi_i}\) is a finite sum of such generators, the
functions \(g_\varepsilon(t)=t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) form the standard
contractive approximate unit for the hereditary algebra they generate.
Polynomial approximation to \(g_\varepsilon^{1/2}\) by polynomials with zero
constant term writes \(g_\varepsilon(s)\) as a norm limit of \(c^*c\) with
\(c\in\mathcal C_U\), and the product formula above makes each \(c^*c\) a
positive coefficient from a finite direct sum of finite fusions.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[intersection of boundary supports]\label{lem:intersection}
Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  For \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
        0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I .                                  \tag{3.1}
\]
Consequently, if \(I=\cap_{i=1}^mU_i\), then
\[
q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m}
   \in q_I\cA_Mq_I .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_R=H_R(V,V)\) be the graph-product correspondence of
Charlesworth--de Santiago--Hayes--Jekel--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Nelson
(CDHJKN).  Their Theorem 5.4 identifies
\(H_R\cong L^2M\otimes_{M_R}L^2M\), and Proposition 5.5 gives
\[
        H_A\otimes_M H_B\cong
        \bigoplus_{C\subset A\cap B}H_C^{\oplus m(A,B,C)} .        \tag{3.2}
\]
Since \(H_A\cong\overline{H_A}\), induction on the number of factors shows
that a finite fusion with labels \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) decomposes as a direct sum
of \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n\).

Take positive coefficients \(h=T_\xi^*T_\xi\) from finite direct sums of
\(\cF_W\) and \(k=T_\eta^*T_\eta\) from finite direct sums of \(\cF_U\).  Then
\[
        hkh=T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}^{\,*}
             T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}.                    \tag{3.3}
\]
By the label induction, the vector in (3.3) lies in a direct sum of
\(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset I\).  Lemma \ref{lem:dsupport}, and
\(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\), imply \(hkh\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).

Choose the contractive approximate units \(h_\alpha\subset X_{M_W}\) and
\(k_\beta\subset X_{M_U}\) supplied by Lemma \ref{lem:dsupport}; by the
preceding paragraph and norm-closedness,
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).  If
\(\phi\in A_M^{\sharp_J}\) vanishes on \(X_{M_I}\), Lemma \ref{lem:support}
gives \(\widetilde\phi(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\alpha)=0\).  In \(\cA_M\),
\(h_\alpha\to q_W\) and \(k_\beta\to q_U\) strongly*, and multiplication is
separately normal on bounded sets.  Hence first letting \(\alpha\) tend to
infinity and then \(\beta\) gives
\[
        \widetilde\phi(q_Wq_Uq_W)=0 .
\]
All normal functionals annihilating \(X_{M_I}\) therefore vanish on
\(q_Wq_Uq_W\), so Lemma \ref{lem:support} places this positive contraction in
\(q_I\cA_Mq_I\), proving (3.1).

The palindromic statement follows by induction: if the product for
\(U_1,\ldots,U_{m-1}\) is a positive contraction supported by
\(q_J\), \(J=\cap_{i<m}U_i\), then compression by \(q_{U_m}\) is dominated by
\(q_{U_m}q_Jq_{U_m}\), which is supported by \(q_{J\cap U_m}\) by (3.1).
\end{proof}

\section*{From finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
\(X_1,\ldots,X_m\) be boundary pieces, \(q_i=q_{X_i}\), and suppose
\[
        R:=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{4.1}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to every \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume not.  By DKEP Lemma 8.5, there is an \(N\)-central state \(\omega\) on
\(\widetilde S(N)\) with \(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\), set \(q=q_i\).  Since
\(q\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\), for \(y\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\),
\[
 [q^\perp yq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[y,a]q^\perp=0,\qquad a\in JNJ,
\]
so \(q^\perp yq^\perp\in\widetilde S(N)\).  If \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), then
\(y\mapsto\omega(q^\perp yq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is an \(N\)-central state
on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  Its restriction to \(N\) is a normal central
state on the factor \(N\), hence is \(\tau\), contradicting relative proper
proximality.  Thus \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for every \(i\).

Extend \(\omega\) to a state on the C\(^*\)-algebra \(\cA_N\).  In the GNS
representation, each \(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector, hence \(\omega(R)=1\).
Since \(0\le R\le q_0\), we get \(\omega(q_0)=1\).  DKEP Lemma 8.5 uses the
canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\longrightarrow
        (\mathbb K(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*=q_0\cA_Nq_0
\]
induced by the normal-bidual quotient; it satisfies \(\Theta_0(1)=q_0\) and
\(\Theta_0(x)=xq_0\) for \(x\in N\).  Since \(q_0\cA_Nq_0\subset
\widetilde S(N)\), the state \(\psi(T)=\omega(\Theta_0(T))\) is \(N\)-central
on \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\), and
\(\psi(x)=\omega(xq_0)=\omega(x)=\tau(x)\) for \(x\in N\).  This is a Connes
hypertrace, contradicting non-amenability of \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations and \(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).
Assume that \(A\) has a trace-zero unitary \(a\), and that \(P\) has unitaries
\(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\) and \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\).  The AFP normal
form is
\[
 L^2N=L^2B\oplus\!\!\bigoplus_{n\ge1,\ i_1\ne\cdots\ne i_n}
 H_{i_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n},\quad i_j\in\{P,Q\}.
\]
Let \(p\) and \(q\) be the projections onto the summands whose first letter is
\(P\), respectively \(Q\); then \(1=e_B+p+q\).  If \(x\in P\ominus B\), right
multiplication by \(x\) preserves the first letter except for the vacuum and
one-letter \(P\)-words, and
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJ e_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0.             \tag{5.1}
\]
The two terms in (5.1) are in the \(N,JNJ\)-bimodule generated by \(e_B\), so
they lie in \(X_B\).  For \(x\in Q\ominus B\) the same statement holds with
\(p\) and \(q\) interchanged, while \(B\) commutes with \(p,q\).  Since these
subspaces generate \(N\), DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) with normal
restriction to \(N\).  Left multiplication by \(w_i\in P\ominus B\) maps
\(qL^2N\) into \(pL^2N\), and
\(q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0\) because \(w_1^*w_2\in P\ominus B\).  Thus
\(w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p\).  Similarly \(a p a^*\le q\), since
\(a\in Q\ominus B\).  Centrality gives
\[
        2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q),
\]
so \(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).

The unitary \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: every non-zero power is an alternating
reduced word in centered \(P\)- and \(Q\)-letters, hence \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for
\(n\ne0\).  Therefore, for \(m\ne n\),
\[
 (h^me_Bh^{-m})(h^ne_Bh^{-n})
   =h^m e_BE_B(h^{\,n-m})e_Bh^{-n}=0 .
\]
The projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and have the same
\(\varphi\)-value, so that value is \(0\).  Hence
\(\varphi(1)=\varphi(e_B+p+q)=0\), a contradiction.  By DKEP Theorem 6.2,
\(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\), put \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\), and use the standard graph-product
amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{6.1}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose
trace-zero unitaries \(u_r,u_t\) and set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  If
\(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) give
\(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  In both
cases graph-product reduced-word calculus gives
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
A trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) supplies the unitary \(a\) in (6.1).  Proposition
\ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex lies in its own link,
\(\cap_iL_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} gives the
palindromic product of the \(q_{L_{v_i}}\)'s in
\(q_{\emptyset}\cA_Mq_{\emptyset}\).  But \(M_\emptyset=\C\), so
\(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\).

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 implies that a join-irreducible graph product with
\(|V|\ge3\) and trace-zero vertex unitaries is a factor.  CDHJKN Proposition
6.3 implies it is not amenable: the amenable exceptional case would force an
isolated two-point non-edge component of \(\Gamma^c\), impossible because
\(\Gamma^c\) is connected and has at least three vertices.  Thus \(M\) is a
separable non-amenable finite factor.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}, applied to the
finite family \(\{X_{M_{L_v}}:v\in V\}\), proves that \(M\) is properly
proximal.

\begin{thebibliography}{9}

\bibitem{DKEP}
C.~Ding, S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and J.~Peterson,
\emph{Properly proximal von Neumann algebras},
Duke Math. J. \textbf{172} (2023), 2821--2894.

\bibitem{CDHJKN}
I.~Charlesworth, R.~de Santiago, B.~Hayes, D.~Jekel,
S.~Kunnawalkam Elayavalli, and B.~Nelson,
\emph{On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras},
Publ. Res. Inst. Math. Sci. \textbf{61} (2025), 713--762.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}

\section*{Refined normal-bidual support model}

Let \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and \(\mathcal A_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For a
hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra \(Y\subset A_N\) with faithful multiplier actions
of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), DKEP Proposition 2.10 should be used as follows.  The
universal simultaneous normal representation embeds \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) as a
non-unital von Neumann subalgebra of \(\mathcal A_N\).  If \(s(Y)\) is its
unit, then \((Y^{\sharp_J})^*=s(Y)\mathcal A_Ns(Y)\); \(s(Y)\) is the strong*
limit in this representation of any c.a.u. of \(Y\); and the canonical copy of
\(Y\) is weak* dense in this corner.  Thus a functional in
\(A_N^{\sharp_J}\), identified with a normal functional on \(\mathcal A_N\),
annihilates \(s(Y)\mathcal A_Ns(Y)\) iff it annihilates \(Y\).

For a boundary piece \(X\), the equality of supports \(s(X)=s(K_X(N))\) follows
directly from DKEP Theorem 5.6(4), not from the simplified multiplier version.
If \(\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\) vanishes on \(X\), then \(\phi\) vanishes on
\(XA_NX\) because \(X\) is hereditary.  Theorem 5.6(4) then annihilates every
\(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\).  Since DKEP Proposition 3.6 gives
\(K_X(N)\subset K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), \(\phi|_{K_X(N)}=0\).  The converse is
trivial from \(X\subset K_X(N)\).  Hence \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same
annihilator in \(A_N^{\sharp_J}\), so their support projections in
\(\mathcal A_N\) agree.

\section*{Subalgebra coefficient linking identity}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), set \(H={}_NL^2N_B\).  DKEP Example
5.11 identifies the left coefficient boundary of \(H\) with \(X_B\).  For the
fused \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(H\otimes_B\overline H\), elementary bounded
vectors satisfy
\[
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\alpha}^{\,*}T_{\eta\otimes\bar\beta}
   =T_\xi^{\,*}\theta_{\alpha,\beta}T_\eta ,
\]
where \(\theta_{\alpha,\beta}\in\mathbb B(L^2B)\) is the rank-one/right-module
coefficient on \(H\).  Thus coefficients of the fusion lie in the hereditary
algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*\mathbb B(L^2B)T_\eta\), which is exactly the
subalgebra boundary \(X_B\) in DKEP Example 3.4.  Taking
\(\alpha=\beta=\widehat 1\) recovers \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), giving the reverse
inclusion.  The tracial conjugation is implemented on algebraic tensors by
\(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes_BJ\xi\); the balanced
relation is respected because \(J\) interchanges left and right multiplication.

\section*{Finite-boundary hypertrace compression}

If \(q=q_X\) is a boundary support and \(y\in\widetilde S_X(N)\), then
\(q^\perp yq^\perp\in\widetilde S(N)\): \(q\) commutes with \(N,JNJ\), and
\([q^\perp yq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[y,a]q^\perp=0\) for \(a\in JNJ\), since
\([y,a]\in q\mathcal A_Nq\).  Hence a central state \(\omega\) on
\(\widetilde S(N)\) with \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\) compresses to a central state on
\(\widetilde S_X(N)\).  On \(N_+\) the compressed functional is dominated by a
scalar multiple of the trace, so it is normal; if \(N\) is a factor, normalized
centrality forces it to be the trace.

For the compact support \(q_0\), DKEP Lemma 8.5 supplies the canonical
\(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map

=q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\), induced by the normal-bidual quotient, with

state on \(\mathcal A_N\) satisfies \(\omega(q_0)=1\), then
\(\omega\circ\Theta_0\) is a Connes hypertrace extending \(\tau\).
"""
